Tesla VP of AI Ashok Elswami confirmed Tesla vehicles have a separate audio model for detecting environmental sounds like sirens, with full voice command integration coming but not yet deployed due to safety testing requirements
Grok voice is integrated into more than 2 million Tesla vehicles
Grok AI assistant rolled out to Tesla vehicles in the UK, Ireland, Germany, Switzerland, Austria, Italy, France, Portugal, and Spain.
FSD version 14.3 is described as a '10x larger model' (1,000% bigger than the current version).
Tesla's AI5 chip is entering production in late 2026, described as roughly equivalent to an Nvidia H100 in a package that fits behind a glove box.
Tesla FSD version 14.2.2.5 successfully navigated a large highway flood puddle spanning nearly two lanes, unlike Waymo which stops functioning in heavy rain or snow due to LiDAR scatter.
Tesla's Colossus 2 data center is projected to reach 1 million H100 equivalents. Tesla's own Cortex 1 and Cortex 2 data centers are in development.
Tesla confirmed FSD will accept natural language voice commands, with Grok LLM integration underway for vehicle controls.
FSD Supervised responds to hand gestures from people outside the vehicle, confirmed by Tesla Europe and Elon Musk.
Tesla discontinued outright FSD purchases in favor of subscriptions, interpreted as a signal that a major software release is imminent.

Tesla nearly doubled its Samsung AI6 chip wafer order: additional 24,000 wafers per month, bringing total to 40,000 wafers per month; original contract was 16,000 wafers per month worth $17 billion over 8 years through 2033.
Samsung AI6 chip built on 2-nanometer process; Samsung's $37 billion fabrication facility in Taylor, Texas is approximately 40 minutes from Giga Texas.
40,000 Samsung wafers per month could yield an estimated 4 to 8 million AI6 chips per month.
Ashok Elluswami (Tesla VP of AI) publicly commented that upcoming FSD tech 'will make it even more so' transformative; Elon Musk added 'most people still have no idea.'
Tesla runs a dual-foundry chip strategy: TSMC handles AI5, Samsung handles AI6, eliminating single points of failure.
Tesla AI4 chip cost: $650 per unit. Each AI4-equipped Tesla has two chips. FSD operates at 36 Hz (36 vision-to-control cycles per second).
Tesla and xAI operate four of the world's largest supercomputers: Cortex 1, Cortex 2, Colossus 1, and Colossus 2.
Elon Musk confirmed FSD V14.3 is in testing and will roll out widely 'in a few weeks.' Musk described v14.3 as 'the last big piece of the puzzle' with AI reasoning capability for reading road signs.
FSD v14.3 expected to iterate through multiple point releases (14.3.1, 14.3.2, etc.) over 3-4 months before achieving wide capability.
Ashok Elluswami at Scale ML 2026 stated: 'The self-driving problem is not a sensor problem. It's an AI problem. The cameras have enough information already.'
Lemonade Insurance data shows Tesla FSD is two times safer than human drivers, offering a 50% insurance discount to FSD users in Arizona and Oregon, with eight more states planned for 2026
Tesla's internal data claims FSD vehicles average 5.1 million miles per major collision versus 699,000 miles for human drivers, roughly seven times safer
Tesla's unsupervised robotaxi safety streak stands at nearly 600,000 miles without a severe incident, compared to average human driver having a serious accident every 300,000 miles

Lemonade insurance launched 'autonomous car insurance' offering 50% off per-mile rates when FSD is engaged, connecting directly to the vehicle's onboard computer to verify FSD engagement in real time.

Former head of autonomous driving at Huawei publicly stated Tesla's FSD is 'generations ahead' of Chinese competitors, revealing that Chinese autonomous driving systems are hybrid systems combining end-to-end models with rule-based components.
Safety analyst 'Reigns OnX' projects Tesla's robotaxi incident rate will cross human-equivalent safety threshold (~700,000 miles per at-fault incident) around March 15, 2026.
Tesla Austin robotaxi fleet miles-per-incident doubled from approximately 200,000 (December 2025) to 417,000 (January 2026).
Nine reported NHTSA incidents across approximately 800,000 robotaxi miles, most at speeds of 2-8 mph, none resulting in injuries.
15 total robotaxi incidents reported since Austin robotaxi launch in June 2025. Safety doubled from December to January 2026.

Tesla announced 8.2 billion FSD-supervised miles driven with 7x fewer major collisions, 7x fewer minor collisions, and 5x fewer off-highway collisions vs US average. FSD-supervised vehicles average 5.3 million miles between major collisions versus 660,000 miles US average.
Tesla announced 8 billion cumulative FSD miles driven, approaching the 10 billion milestone.
Tesla has accumulated 8.2 billion FSD supervised miles as of early March 2026, with 10 billion miles cited as Elon Musk's threshold for unsupervised self-driving.
Analyst projects Tesla will reach 10 billion FSD supervised miles by June 22, 2026.

Tesla FSD supervised shows 7x fewer major collisions, 7x fewer minor collisions, and 5x fewer off-highway collisions compared to average U.S. driving. One crash per 5.3 million miles vs. 660,000 miles for U.S. average.

Tesla's Austin robotaxi fleet logged 14 NHTSA-reported incidents since launching in June 2025, across 225,000 miles driven in the final two months of 2025. Zero hospitalizations, zero airbag deployments, no towing required.
David Moss drove 11,000 miles on Tesla FSD in the U.S. with zero disengagements.

Tesla FSD supervised crash rate: 1 crash per 5 million miles; Tesla manual with active safety: 1 per 2 million miles; Tesla manual without active safety: 1 per 855,000 miles; US national average: 1 per 600,000 miles. Crash definition requires airbag deployment, seatbelt pyrotechnic activation, or HV battery fuse trigger.
43,000 annual U.S. traffic deaths and 150,000 serious injuries cited as the human driving baseline.
An average of 110 people die every day on U.S. roads from human driving; 32,000 lives could be saved per year if supervised FSD were universally available.

Five Tesla robotaxi incidents reported to NHTSA in January 2026: four occurred under 4 mph in parking lots, one at 17 mph hitting a fixed object; no airbags deployed, no hospitalizations, no towing required.
GM announced beginning autonomous vehicle data collection in 2026 with 100 test vehicles, compared to Tesla's 8.4 billion cumulative FSD miles dating back to 2012.
Tesla began capturing vehicle camera data from every vehicle sold starting in 2012.
FSD Supervised achieves 5.3 million miles between major collisions versus a US average of 660,000 miles; 7x fewer major collisions, 7x fewer minor collisions, 5x fewer off-highway collisions.
Approximately 42,000-44,000 Americans die annually in road accidents; FSD Supervised could save an estimated 32,000 lives per year if universally adopted.
Tesla fleet generates over 20 million miles of real-world visual data per day, with 8.44 billion cumulative FSD miles logged.
Tesla has logged approximately 8.5 billion FSD miles as of early March 2026, adding 20 million miles per day. Cumulative total expected to cross 10 billion miles by midsummer 2026.
Lemonade Insurance announced a 50% discount on premiums when Tesla FSD is in use.
Tesla's NHTSA robotaxi incident report for January 15-February 15 showed only 1 incident (low-speed contact at approximately 1 mph), with 15 total incidents since the program began.
Tesla FSD supervised safety data shows the system is approximately 9x safer than unassisted driving.
Tesla executive Yan Tasai confirmed Tesla does not require remote human checks for its autonomous vehicles, unlike Waymo which uses remote human operators.
43,000 annual US traffic fatalities cited, with an additional 150,000+ seriously injured each year.
FSD one-time purchase price in US is approximately $8,000 with $100/month subscription alternative; Australia's one-time purchase option ends March 2026
Only about 10% of Tesla owners consider FSD a critical purchase factor; the number is far lower among non-Tesla buyers
FSD became subscription-only in the US as of February 14, 2026; the one-time purchase option (previously $8,000-$12,000) was eliminated.
Tesla currently has 1.1 million paid FSD users, with roughly 330,000 on subscriptions generating approximately $33 million in monthly recurring revenue ($400 million annually).
FSD is currently doing about 19-20 million miles per day; roughly 10% of miles driven by Tesla owners in the US are on FSD.
Elon Musk's compensation package includes a milestone of 10 million active FSD subscriptions.
Tesla owners are averaging more than 20 million FSD miles per day in Q1 2026, up 30% from a few months prior — over 13,000 miles per minute.
Tesla owners in South Korea drove over 5 million miles (8 million kilometers) using FSD Supervised in just 100 days of availability.
A price of EUR 9 per month for FSD was found in hidden code on Tesla's European website.
FSD US subscription price is $99/month (~$108 with state taxes). Take rates reach roughly 50% when offered free but drop to 10-20% when priced.
FSD subscription for Cybercab fleet owners priced at $199/month in the financial model.
Over 4 million Tesla vehicles are equipped with the AI4 chip.
The Self-Drive Act of 2026 passed a House committee 12-to-11, introduced by Representatives Bob Latta and Debbie Dingell on February 5, with best-case timeline for becoming law by summer 2026
Current federal AV exemption cap is 2,500 vehicles without steering wheels/pedals; the Self-Drive Act would raise this to approximately 90,000-95,000 units
California regulators require a minimum of 50,000-60,000 supervised miles before approving driverless operation.
NHTSA caps fully autonomous vehicles (no steering wheel or pedals) at approximately 2,500-2,900 units. The SAFE Drive Act could raise the cap to 95,000 units.
States with explicit robotaxi approval include California, Florida, Georgia, Texas, and Arizona, collectively covering over 100 million people (more than a third of US population).
Transportation Secretary Shawn Duffy streamlined the NHTSA exemption application process for autonomous vehicles.

Waymo submitted a proposal to California DMV to ban Tesla from using 'driverless,' 'self-driving,' or 'robotaxi' in marketing. Tesla filed a lawsuit against the California DMV to reverse a prior ruling on autopilot/FSD terminology.

NHTSA held a major autonomous vehicle safety public meeting on March 10, 2026 at US DOT headquarters. Current US autonomous vehicle exemption cap is 2,500 units, with additional exemptions available through a streamlined process updated June 2025.
NHTSA deadlines: March 9, 2026 for Tesla to submit robotaxi incident data; March 10, 2026 full-day NHTSA autonomous vehicle event at DOT headquarters.

The 2,500 Cybercab production cap only applies to manufacturers seeking an FMVSS exemption; a 2022 NHTSA ruling allows unlimited production for autonomous vehicles that can self-certify full FMVSS compliance without steering wheels or pedals.
Proposed congressional increase to the FMVSS autonomous vehicle exemption cap is approximately 90,000-95,000 vehicles per year.
NHTSA administrator Jonathan Morrison stated the agency wants to 'remove unnecessary, unintended barriers' to self-driving technology.

US Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy hosted a forum at DOT headquarters with Waymo, Zoox, and Tesla. Duffy questioned whether autonomous vehicles need steering wheels and said the department needs to 'rethink some of our requirements.'

2022 NHTSA rule change already permits vehicles without steering wheels and pedals as long as they pass all safety requirements. The 2,500-vehicle exemption only applies to vehicles that cannot meet standard safety certification.
Approximately seven US states including Texas, Florida, Georgia, Illinois, and Arizona allow Tesla to deploy robotaxis at scale without significant regulatory hurdles.
Tesla's Cybercab has regulatory safety certification labels on its doors, confirming it is a road-legal certified vehicle.

U.S. Secretary of Transportation Sean Duffy hosted the first-ever National AV Safety Forum and announced NHTSA will revise Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards to accommodate vehicles with no steering wheel, pedals, or human controls.
CyberCab self-certified under existing FMVSS standards (not NHTSA exemption route), removing any federal cap on production/deployment volume.
Tesla activated a dedicated FSD training AI data center in China, confirmed by VP Grace Tao on February 6, 2026, to process local driving data under China's data localization laws
Tesla targeted February 2026 to demonstrate FSD compliance to Dutch regulator RDW under UN Regulation 171, which could unlock approval across all 27 EU countries
Elon Musk stated FSD is expected to receive approval in the Netherlands around March 20, 2026. FSD supervised has already begun in the UAE.
FSD Supervised road trials officially began in Abu Dhabi, UAE. FSD is active in Canada, Mexico, Australia, New Zealand, China (narrow release), and Korea.
FSD supervised launched in the Netherlands on March 20. EU countries tend to follow each other's regulatory lead.
FSD is currently active in Korea, Australia, and China in addition to North America.
FSD supervised approval in the Netherlands is expected by April 2026. Czech Republic and Germany have already passed laws allowing certified Level 3 systems on highways.
UNECE is developing new legislation to create a global framework for autonomous driving, which will govern robotaxi deployment in Europe.
Tesla careers page at Giga Berlin listing robotaxi/autonomous vehicle engineer positions, signaling European deployment potentially 12-18 months away.
Tesla hiring vehicle operators in Croatia, Greece, Portugal, Slovenia, and the Netherlands on one-year fixed-term contracts for FSD data collection.
Elon Musk stated he expects FSD approval in the Netherlands around March 20, 2026.
FSD testing in Japan began August 2025 on Tokyo public roads with Model 3 and Model Y; Tesla aims for FSD approval in Japan within 2026.
Ireland signed a statutory instrument legalizing Level 2 autonomous vehicles on public roads.
A new UNECE autonomous driving framework adopted in January 2026 replaced rigid prescriptive rules with an outcome-based safety case approach.
Dutch Vehicle Authority (RDW) already testing FSD on public roads including busy city centers ahead of potential March 20, 2026 approval.

Japanese Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport, and Tourism approved regulatory measures allowing FSD activation via software updates; Tesla Japan subsidiary president stated goal to implement FSD in Japan during 2026.
FSD supervised road trials are active in Abu Dhabi, Tokyo, and Norway, which has approved public road testing.

Tesla FSD Supervised expected to receive Dutch RDW approval around March 20, 2026, making the Netherlands the first European country to approve FSD. Phase 1 restricted to highways, major intercity roads, and pre-validated urban routes.
Tesla is hiring autopilot vehicle operators in Croatia, Greece, Portugal, Slovenia, and the Netherlands.
Tesla FSD in Japan targeting launch by end of 2026, with testing active in Tokyo since August 2025 and roughly 40,000 eligible vehicles on Japanese roads.

FSD EU approval mechanism uses an EU Article 39 exemption which allows other EU member states to recognize the Dutch approval under mutual recognition frameworks; Belgium, Germany, and Nordic countries could follow rapidly.

facts.fact: Tesla's job board shows 53 new Cybercab manufacturing roles at Giga Texas covering assembly, injection molding, and castings
Cybercab production start confirmed for April 2026 at Giga Texas, described by Musk as 'an all-new product and radical redesign of car manufacturing to achieve five times higher production rate'
Cybercab manufacturing target: one vehicle every 10 seconds, only 80 major components, eventual target of 4 million units per year
Cybercab production projection: low end 20,000 units, high end 40,000 units by December 31, 2026, using Cybertruck ramp as conservative benchmark
Tesla is actively recruiting injection molding engineers, process engineers, maintenance supervisors, and equipment technicians specifically for the Cybercab.
CyberCab has no pedals or steering wheel; it can only be deployed as a robotaxi, not sold as a conventional vehicle.
First CyberCab rolled off the production line at Giga Texas on February 17, 2026, ahead of the April start-of-production date. Approximately 420 Tesla employees were present.
Projected 2026 total Cybercab production: 10,000 to 20,000 units, possibly up to 30,000 in an optimistic case.
CyberCab production line uses Tesla's novel 'unboxed' assembly model, with a target takt time of sub-10 seconds per unit.
Grok 4.2 from xAI introduces a multi-agent parallel reasoning architecture and projected 20,910 cumulative Cybercab units for 2026 and 230,000 for 2027.
Tesla Model Y production rate: 1 unit every 35 seconds. Cybercab target: 1 every 10 seconds, eventually 1 every 5 seconds.
CyberCab features a completely redesigned 48-volt electrical architecture with less than one-third the wiring complexity of the CyberTruck.

CyberCab uses reaction injection molding for exterior panels (no steel or aluminum on the outside), unplated aluminum connectors, and a new motor with significantly higher power output per pound than any Tesla motor to date.
CyberCab is the first production vehicle designed to work in both right-hand and left-hand drive markets without modification.
Cybercab mass production at Giga Texas targeted for April 2026; drone footage shows crash-testing units exiting the factory.

Tesla's unboxed manufacturing system for Cybercab is claimed capable of producing one vehicle every six seconds, versus a conventional rate of one every 30 seconds — a roughly 5x improvement in throughput.
Single Cybercab production line could theoretically output around 5 million units annually.
Cybercab wireless charging efficiency reported at 93%.
FCC granted Tesla a waiver for ultra-wideband wireless EV charging positioning technology for Cybercab precise charger alignment.
Approximately 100 Cybercab units confirmed inside Giga Texas in various production stages, consistent with 6-8 week pilot phase before April start of production.

Cybercab specs: two-passenger, no steering wheel or pedals, 200-mile range, 35 kWh battery, inductive charging, target price under $30,000. Elon cited 10-second cycle time target, eventual goal of one unit every 5 seconds per line.
25 Cybercabs documented at Giga Texas on March 3, 2026: 15 sent to crash testing, 4 at factory exit, rest loaded onto covered transports likely heading to California.
Tesla Cybercabs are rolling off the line for crash testing as of early March 2026. FSD approval in the Netherlands appears imminent.
Cybercab production launch expected in April 2026. Elon Musk projected 30,000 Cybercabs production in 2026.

First production Cybercab rolled off the Giga Texas line on February 17, 2026, several weeks ahead of schedule. 25 units visible by drone footage as of early March, approximately 10 at crash testing facility.

Cybercab will be sold directly to consumers for under $30,000. Current NHTSA rules limit vehicles without steering wheels to 2,500 units per year without exemption, but Tesla may bypass this through full safety certification.
Board chairwoman Robyn Denholm confirmed a fallback plan to add steering wheel and pedals to Cybercab if regulators require it.
Giga Berlin identified as next Cybercab production site, with first hardware engineering role posted March 4, 2026.
30 Cybercabs observed being loaded into transport trucks at Giga Texas for nationwide testing; 15 sent to crash test facilities.
Cybercab interior features a 21-inch center screen, the largest display in any Tesla vehicle.

Over 100 Cybercabs built and shipped out of Giga Texas by mid-March 2026, roughly one month before official April start-of-production. Projected ramp: 1,000+ units/month by May, 2,000/month by June-July, 5,000/month by Q3/early Q4. Full-year 2026 estimate: 20,000-40,000 units.

Cybercab body panels made via thermoplastic injection molding using four machines. Color is molded directly into plastic pellets ('molded-in color'), eliminating the traditional paint process entirely.
Cybercab has only 3 pieces of glass (windshield and two door windows) compared to 15 on a Rivian R1S.
Cybercab uses Tesla's 'unbox' manufacturing process with five major parallel sub-assemblies built simultaneously on separate lines across multiple factory floors, then joined together.
Tesla CyberCab program lead Eric publicly stated Tesla looks forward to 'launching them at scale this year' in response to Secretary Duffy at the AV Safety Forum.
30+ CyberCabs were spotted lined up at Giga Texas, and additional units were on display at a South by Southwest pop-up event in downtown Austin.
CyberCab production at Giga Texas is expected to begin in April 2026, confirmed by Morgan Stanley after visiting Giga Texas.

CyberCab design refinements include: window switches relocated under center screen, redesigned charge port with full surround gasket, hard plastic interior replacing cloth for robotic cleaning with drain plug added, and seats at wheelchair height with braille controls.
April 2026 expected catalysts: Cybercab production begins, FSD V14.3 launches, X Money early public access, possible next-gen Roadster unveil.
Jeff Lutz projected CyberCab production ramp: approximately 100 units in April, hundreds/month in May, ~1,000/month by June, for a few thousand total in Q2 2026.
Tesla rental program offers 7-day rentals including FSD and Supercharger access for approximately $60/day, expanding to new cities with dedicated staff
Tesla rental program expanded to Texas, Massachusetts, Tennessee, and Arizona, with Michigan and Florida filings submitted. Pricing: $60/day for Model 3/Y, $75 for Cybertruck, $90 for Model S/X.
Tesla rental program pricing: $60/day for Model 3/Y, $70 for S/X, all-inclusive with supercharging and FSD; $250 purchase credit within 7 days of rental.
90% of all ride-hail trips involve one person, 5% involve two people, and 5% require more than two passengers.
85% of car trips in North America have just one or two people; average vehicle occupancy per trip is 1.5 people.
Tesla is hiring robotaxi pricing software engineers to build dynamic and surge pricing algorithms using LLM and agentic AI
Tesla robotaxi currently charges approximately $1.99 per kilometer (~$2.50/mile) in San Francisco, with average fares around $8.17 per ride.
ARK Invest research pegs the U.S. robotaxi market at $3.9 trillion annually at equilibrium, or $2.8 trillion even if users never give up personal vehicles.
ARK research indicates Tesla can earn profit at $0.50 per mile for robotaxi. At $0.50/mile, the TAM approaches $3 trillion — a 6x expansion versus current ride-hail pricing.
Americans spend $1.6 trillion annually on direct driving costs and $4.4 trillion in forfeited labor time — $6 trillion total effective cost.
Uber's effective cost per mile in Austin: $5.44 over 32 miles ($177.83 total); a 3.2-mile round trip during peak hours cost $84.94.
Tesla robotaxi Austin base rate raised from $1 to over $3; a two-mile trip now costs $5 versus prior $3.
Tesla robotaxi pricing as of March 7, 2026: $3.25 base fare plus $1 per mile.
Current Austin robotaxi base fare: $3 with $1.40/mile, compared to traditional rideshare at $2-4/mile.
Tesla robotaxi pricing in Austin increased to $3 base rate and $1.40 per mile (up from $1.00/mile), with surge pricing up to $2/mile at night.
Uber internal 2022 presentation data: a $1 decrease in per-mile pricing results in a 10x expansion of total addressable market for ride-hailing.
Tesla's internal robotaxi cost target is reportedly 25-50 cents per mile.
Tesla robotaxi cost per mile at scale projected at under 50 cents; suggested consumer pricing of $1-2 per mile.
Cybercab estimated manufacturing cost per unit is approximately $25,000 at 100,000 unit volume, ballooning to $200,000 at 3,000 unit volume
Estimated Cybercab manufacturing capex is roughly $4 billion, amortized over 8 years equals $500 million in fixed costs per year
10,000 robotaxi vehicles would generate roughly $400 million in net income, about 10% of Tesla's 2025 net income of $4 billion
CyberCab projected margin is approximately $30,000 per unit per year when deployed as a robotaxi, versus $6,000-$7,000 margin per Model Y sale.
Cybercab production cost estimated at approximately $20,000 per unit to produce.
Elon Musk confirmed CyberCab will be sold to a customer for under $30,000 before end of 2026.
CyberCab retail price expected around $25,000.
CyberCab owner economics model at $1/mile: 30% utilization yields ~$32,000 gross revenue and ~$14,000 gross profit annually; 65% utilization yields ~$40,000 gross profit annually.

A single Tesla robotaxi is modeled to generate $69,000-$93,000 annually in margin (at $1.60-$2.00/mile revenue, $0.50/mile COGS, 200 miles/day, 26 days/month) — equivalent to the margin from selling 12 Model Y vehicles per year.
Scaling projections: 20,000 robotaxis by December 2026 would add ~50% to Tesla's annual FCF; 200,000 in 2027 would add ~$18.7 billion FCF (522% increase); 500,000 in 2028 would add ~$50 billion FCF.
Tesla's $4 billion manufacturing investment in the Cybercab production line at Giga Texas.
Chamath Palihapitiya stated he would buy and deploy large fleets of Cybercabs, projecting positive free cash flow in under two years.

Cybercab robotaxi financial model at 30% utilization and $1.40/mile: approximately $80,000 gross revenue per vehicle per year; approximately $23,000 net profit to owner; Tesla receives approximately $28,500/year per vehicle on network at 35% take rate.
Cybercab manufacturing cost target is approximately $15,000 at scale, compared to approximately $45,000 COGS for comparable Chinese competitors.
Waymo ordered 50,000 Hyundai Ioniq 5 vehicles by 2028 at approximately $50,000 per vehicle, totaling $2.5 billion excluding sensor upfit costs
Waymo currently operates approximately 2,500 vehicles and plans only ~3,000 by end of 2026; New Street Research found Waymo cannot profitably exceed ~500 vehicles per city
Toyota and Pony AI plan to deploy 1,000 BZ4X robotaxi units in 2026 across China tier-one cities, targeting 3,000 total fleet by year-end
Xpeng CEO promised FSD performance equivalent to Tesla's v14 by end of summer 2026
Waymo paying DoorDashers $11 ($6.25 guaranteed plus $5 upon verified completion) to close doors left open by passengers in Atlanta
Starlink has approximately 9,000 satellites currently deployed and 10 million subscribers.
Starlink V3 satellites carry 16-64x the bandwidth of existing satellites, with 64 deployed per Starship mission.
Pony AI and GAC Toyota debuted a mass-produced robotaxi based on the Volkswagen ID. Buzz platform.
Waymo, Uber, and Lyft pricing in San Francisco (June 2024 study): $4-$42/mile for short trips under a mile, $4-$6/mile for trips between 2.7-5.8 miles.
The Boring Company's Nashville tunnel was approved unanimously by city council, entirely privately funded at no cost to taxpayers.
Waymo operates roughly 3,000 robotaxis across six U.S. cities after more than a decade of operation and billions in Google funding.
Waymo confirmed it maintains roughly 70 remote operators on duty at any given time, split between the Philippines and the U.S.
XPeng holds a robotaxi permit in Guangzhou, China and plans to begin robotaxi operations by August 2025.
XPeng's CEO estimated it could take 5 years to achieve full-width-of-China autonomous driving capability outside cities.
Uber announced $100 million investment in autonomous charging hubs, yielding roughly 2,000 stations at $50,000 each.
Uber announced 'Uber Autonomous Solutions' including training data collection via Lucid fleet, data-enriched mapping, regulatory support, fleet financing, and remote operations for AV partners.
XPeng's XIMO/VA 2.0 vision-only smart driving system licensed to Volkswagen, marking the first large-scale export of core Chinese autonomous driving technology.

Waymo launched invite-only service simultaneously in Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, and Orlando — now operating in 10 U.S. cities total. Waymo loses money on each car beyond approximately 500 per city. Waymo has raised $16 billion but remains unprofitable.

Waive AI (UK startup, founded 2017) raised $1.2 billion from Nvidia, Uber, Nissan, Mercedes-Benz, and Stellantis. Uses end-to-end neural network approach, tested in over 500 cities across Europe, Asia, and North America.
Lucid Motors market cap at $3.2 billion, down from a prior $20 billion.
Zoox self-certified their autonomous vehicle as FMVSS-compliant in July 2022; NHTSA challenged it in March 2023; Zoox granted exemption September 25, 2025 up to 2,500-unit cap.
Apollo Go (Baidu's robotaxi) reached 20 million trips and 190 million fully driverless kilometers with approximately 3,000 vehicles.
Xpeng introduced its second-generation VALA 2.0 autonomous driving system and plans to launch fully driverless robotaxis in Guangzhou later in 2026.

Zoox announced expansion of testing to Dallas and Phoenix, bringing total US testing footprint to 10 cities. Zoox has logged over 1 million autonomous miles and 300,000 riders served across Las Vegas and San Francisco as of late 2025.
China already has 50 cities approved for autonomous robotaxi operation.

Xpeng CEO He Xiaopeng declared China the global leader in autonomous driving, predicting L4 autonomy fully deployed in designated areas in China within 1-3 years. Xpeng has a robotaxi pilot in Guangzhou.
Xpeng's XIMO VA 2.0 autonomous driving system assessed by tester Brian White as comparable to 'a very early version 13' of Tesla FSD.
Huawei described as 'the Intel Inside of FSD in China,' partnering with approximately a dozen EV manufacturers for autonomous driving technology.
Lucid revealed a concept robotaxi called Lunar, a purpose-built two-seater with specs including 5.5-6 miles per kWh efficiency, 42 inches of legroom, and 40% lower operating cost per mile.
Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe stated it could be 2038-2040 before Rivian might simplify its sensor stack and move away from LiDAR for autonomous driving.
Uber and Rivian announced partnership to deploy up to 50,000 R2 robotaxis across 25 cities in US, Canada, and Europe by end of 2031, with Uber investing up to $1.25 billion in Rivian.
Nvidia and Uber announced partnership to deploy robotaxis in LA and San Francisco in H1 2027, expanding to 28 cities across 4 continents by 2028 using Nvidia Drive Hyperion hardware.
Nvidia announced four new automaker partners for Drive Hyperion (BYD, Geely, Nissan, Hyundai), bringing total to 19 partners building approximately 18 million cars per year.
